news_summary: Today’s environmental advancements highlight rapid plant adaptation, innovative solar cell technology, a pivotal shift away from fossil fuels, and breakthroughs in sustainable algae-based materials.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées environnementales actuelles mettent en avant la capacité d'adaptation rapide des plantes, les technologies innovantes en matière de cellules solaires, un tournant décisif dans l'abandon des combustibles fossiles et des percées dans le domaine des matériaux durables à base d'algues.
news_summary_es: Los avances medioambientales actuales ponen de relieve la rápida adaptación de las plantas, la innovadora tecnología de las células solares, un cambio fundamental que deja atrás los combustibles fósiles y los avances en materiales sostenibles a base de algas.

articles.title: Cacti are evolving shockingly fast and scientists just learned why
articles.summary: Cacti may look like slow, stubborn desert survivors, but they’re actually evolving at lightning speed. Scientists studying more than 750 cactus species discovered that what really drives the explosion of new cactus species isn’t flower size or specialized pollinators, but how quickly cactus flowers change shape over time. The finding overturns a long-standing idea dating back to Darwin and reveals deserts as surprisingly dynamic ecosystems where evolution is happening fast.
articles.content:
Cacti are famous for growing slowly, but scientists have discovered that these desert plants are actually evolving at an impressive pace. New research shows that cacti are remarkably quick at forming entirely new species, revealing that deserts are far more dynamic than they may seem.

For decades, biologists believed that pollinators and highly specialized flowers were the main forces behind the development of new plant species. However, researchers at the University of Reading found that cacti follow a different pattern. Instead of flower size or pollinator type driving diversification, the key factor appears to be how rapidly cactus flowers change shape over time.

Rapid Flower Evolution in Cacti

The research team analyzed flower length data from more than 750 cactus species. Flower sizes varied dramatically, ranging from tiny blooms measuring just 2mm to giant flowers reaching 37cm, representing a 185-fold difference in size.

Even with such enormous variation, flower length showed almost no connection to how quickly new cactus species emerged. What did stand out was the speed of floral evolution. Cactus species whose flowers changed shape most rapidly were also far more likely to branch off into new species. Researchers found this pattern consistently across both recent and ancient evolutionary history.

The findings, published in Biology Letters, challenge ideas that date back to Charles Darwin. Darwin's work on orchids suggested that highly specialized flower structures played a major role in the formation of new species.

Jamie Thompson, lead author at the University of Reading, said: "People may think of cacti as tough, slow-growing plants, but our research shows that the cactus family is one of the fastest-evolving plant groups on Earth. Knowing how fast cacti evolve reveals that deserts, often seen as harsh and unchanging, are actually hotbeds of rapid natural change.

"We expected cacti with longer, more specialized flowers to be the ones creating the most new species. Instead, flower size made almost no difference. What matters is how quickly flowers change shape. Cacti whose flowers evolve rapidly are far more likely to split into new species than those whose flowers stay the same, however elaborate they are.

"This result has real implications for conservation. Since flower evolution has helped generate cactus species over millions of years, evolutionary pace should become part of conservation efforts. Although being able to rapidly evolve does not guarantee resilience, especially as the planet is changing faster than most cacti can keep up, it could help predict which species need the most help. Rather than searching for a single trait that predicts which cacti are most at risk, conservationists may need to look at how fast a species is evolving instead."

Mapping the Cactus Family Tree

Cacti include roughly 1,850 known species and rank among the fastest-diversifying plant groups on Earth. Over the last 20 to 35 million years, they have spread widely throughout the Americas.

The study also relied on a newly developed Open Access database called CactEcoDB. Created by Jamie Thompson and ten coauthors from three continents, including six researchers from the University of Reading, the database combines seven years of research on cactus traits, habitats, and evolutionary relationships.

The resource was published in Nature Scientific Data and is expected to help scientists better understand cactus biodiversity and conservation challenges. With nearly one-third of cactus species currently threatened with extinction, researchers say the database will provide an important tool for studying how these plants may respond to climate change in the future.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about rapid evolution in cacti, overturning long-held beliefs and revealing deserts as dynamic ecosystems. It has broad implications for biodiversity conservation and climate change resilience, supported by a comprehensive new database aiding future research and protection efforts.
articles.personality_title: Scientists discover why cacti evolve faster than expected
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Cacti are desert plants known for growing slowly, and scientists long believed that flower size or special pollinators helped create new cactus species.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of Reading studied over 750 cactus species and found that the speed at which cactus flowers change shape, not their size or pollinators, drives the creation of new species. They used a new database called CactEcoDB to analyze flower length and shape evolution.

**Impact** – This discovery challenges old ideas from Darwin’s time and shows deserts are places where evolution happens quickly. It means that how fast cactus flowers evolve can help predict which species might survive or need protection.

**What's next step** – Scientists will use the CactEcoDB database to study cactus biodiversity and help conservation efforts, especially as many cactus species face threats from climate change.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Rapid changes in cactus flower shapes, not size, explain why cacti evolve quickly, revealing deserts as dynamic places for plant evolution.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent pourquoi les cactus évoluent plus vite que prévu
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les cactus sont des plantes du désert connues pour leur croissance lente, et les scientifiques pensaient longtemps que la taille des fleurs ou les pollinisateurs spéciaux créaient de nouvelles espèces de cactus.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de Reading ont étudié plus de 750 espèces de cactus et ont découvert que c’est la rapidité avec laquelle les fleurs de cactus changent de forme, et non leur taille ou leurs pollinisateurs, qui crée de nouvelles espèces. Ils ont utilisé une nouvelle base de données appelée CactEcoDB pour analyser la taille et l’évolution de la forme des fleurs.

**Impact** – Cette découverte remet en cause des idées anciennes datant de l’époque de Darwin et montre que les déserts sont des endroits où l’évolution se produit rapidement. Cela signifie que la vitesse d’évolution des fleurs de cactus peut aider à prévoir quelles espèces pourraient survivre ou avoir besoin de protection.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques utiliseront la base de données CactEcoDB pour étudier la biodiversité des cactus et aider les efforts de conservation, surtout que de nombreuses espèces sont menacées par le changement climatique.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les changements rapides de forme des fleurs de cactus, et non leur taille, expliquent pourquoi les cactus évoluent vite, révélant les déserts comme des lieux dynamiques pour l’évolution des plantes.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren por qué los cactus evolucionan más rápido de lo esperado
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los cactus son plantas del desierto conocidas por crecer lentamente, y los científicos creían que el tamaño de las flores o polinizadores especiales ayudaban a crear nuevas especies.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Reading estudiaron más de 750 especies de cactus y encontraron que la rapidez con que cambian la forma de sus flores, no el tamaño ni los polinizadores, impulsa la creación de nuevas especies. Usaron una nueva base de datos llamada CactEcoDB para analizar el tamaño y la evolución de la forma de las flores.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento desafía ideas antiguas desde la época de Darwin y muestra que los desiertos son lugares donde la evolución ocurre rápidamente. Esto significa que la velocidad con que evolucionan las flores de cactus puede ayudar a predecir qué especies podrían sobrevivir o necesitar protección.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos usarán la base de datos CactEcoDB para estudiar la biodiversidad de los cactus y ayudar en la conservación, especialmente porque muchas especies están amenazadas por el cambio climático.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los rápidos cambios en la forma de las flores de cactus, no su tamaño, explican por qué los cactus evolucionan rápido, mostrando que los desiertos son lugares dinámicos para la evolución de las plantas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a diverse array of cacti with vividly varied and dynamically swirling flower shapes and sizes, arranged like a blossoming family tree against a softly glowing desert landscape at dawn, symbolizing rapid evolution and species branching without any human figures.

articles.title: Stereoelectronic manipulation of ligands for perovskite solar cells
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 13 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10626-0Stereoelectronic manipulation of ligands for perovskite solar cells
articles.content: Interfacial losses at perovskite/charge transport layer heterojunctions persist as a critical barrier to achieving high-performance perovskite solar cells.1-5 While molecular ligands can passivate interfacial vacancy defects, their vertical anchoring geometry compromises charge transport by increasing interfacial transport pathway. Here, we demonstrate that stereoelectronic manipulation of ligand adsorption topology advances interfacial minimum energy loss for efficient and stable perovskite solar cells. By strategically replacing benzene carbons with nitrogen atoms to create pyridine or pyrimidine rings, we design ligands that concurrently anchor to the perovskite through Pb-N coordination bonds and Pb-I-π interactions, endowing a single molecule with dual, synergistic binding modes. This mutually reinforcing stereoelectronic interplay drives thermodynamically favorable planar alignment of ligands, enabling atomic-scale defect mitigation while maintaining sub-nanometer-scale charge transfer across the interface. The optimized interfacial architecture achieves a stabilized power output of 26.85%, with certificated reverse-scan and forward-scan efficiencies of 27.41% and 26.35%, respectively. Furthermore, the solar modules exhibit exceptional operational stability, retaining 85.8% of initial module efficiency after 258 days of outdoor real‐time field testing.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in perovskite solar cell technology that improves efficiency and operational stability, which has broad implications for renewable energy and climate action. The detailed explanation of the ligand manipulation and the impressive efficiency and stability results demonstrate a meaningful advancement with potential large-scale environmental benefits.
articles.personality_title: New ligand design boosts efficiency and stability of perovskite solar cells
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Perovskite solar cells are a promising type of solar technology that could be cheaper and easier to make than current options. However, these cells often lose power because of problems where different layers meet inside the cell.

**What happened** – Scientists found a new way to fix this problem by changing the shape and chemistry of molecules called ligands that sit between the layers. They replaced parts of these molecules with nitrogen atoms to create special rings, allowing the ligands to stick better and lay flat on the surface. This design improves the connection between layers, helping electricity flow more smoothly.

**Impact** – This new method made solar cells with a high power efficiency of nearly 27%, which is very good for perovskite technology. The solar modules also stayed strong and kept most of their power after running outside for about 8 and a half months. This means the cells are not only efficient but also last a long time in real conditions.

**What's next step** – Researchers will likely work on scaling up this design to make bigger solar panels and test them in more environments. This could help bring better and more affordable solar energy to everyday use.

**One-sentence takeaway** – By redesigning molecules at the layer interfaces, scientists greatly improved both the power and durability of perovskite solar cells, moving them closer to practical use.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle conception de ligands améliore l'efficacité et la stabilité des cellules solaires pérovskites
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les cellules solaires pérovskites sont une technologie solaire prometteuse, moins coûteuse et plus facile à fabriquer que les options actuelles. Cependant, elles perdent souvent de l'énergie à cause de problèmes aux interfaces entre leurs différentes couches.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des scientifiques ont trouvé un moyen de résoudre ce problème en modifiant la forme et la chimie de molécules appelées ligands, situées entre les couches. Ils ont remplacé des parties de ces molécules par des atomes d'azote pour créer des anneaux spéciaux, permettant aux ligands de mieux s'accrocher et de se placer à plat sur la surface. Cette conception améliore la connexion entre les couches, facilitant le passage de l'électricité.

**Impact** – Cette nouvelle méthode a permis d'obtenir des cellules solaires avec une efficacité énergétique proche de 27 %, ce qui est très bon pour la technologie pérovskite. Les modules solaires sont également restés performants et ont conservé la plupart de leur puissance après environ 8 mois et demi d'utilisation en extérieur. Cela montre que les cellules sont à la fois efficaces et durables dans des conditions réelles.

**Étape suivante** – Les chercheurs vont probablement travailler à la production de panneaux solaires plus grands avec ce design et les tester dans différents environnements. Cela pourrait aider à rendre l'énergie solaire meilleure et plus accessible au quotidien.

**En une phrase** – En repensant les molécules aux interfaces, les scientifiques ont nettement amélioré la puissance et la durabilité des cellules solaires pérovskites, les rapprochant d’une utilisation pratique.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo diseño de ligandos mejora la eficiencia y estabilidad de las células solares de perovskita
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las células solares de perovskita son una tecnología prometedora que podría ser más barata y fácil de fabricar que las opciones actuales. Sin embargo, estas células a menudo pierden energía debido a problemas en las uniones entre sus capas.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos encontraron una forma de solucionar este problema cambiando la forma y química de moléculas llamadas ligandos que se encuentran entre las capas. Reemplazaron partes de estas moléculas con átomos de nitrógeno para crear anillos especiales, lo que permite que los ligandos se adhieran mejor y se alineen planos sobre la superficie. Este diseño mejora la conexión entre capas, ayudando a que la electricidad fluya mejor.

**Impacto** – Este nuevo método logró células solares con una eficiencia de casi 27 %, lo cual es muy bueno para la tecnología de perovskita. Los módulos solares también mostraron gran resistencia, manteniendo la mayoría de su potencia después de unos 8 meses y medio de uso al aire libre. Esto significa que las células son eficientes y duraderas en condiciones reales.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores probablemente trabajarán para fabricar paneles solares más grandes con este diseño y probarlos en más lugares. Esto podría ayudar a que la energía solar sea mejor y más accesible para todos.

**Una frase clave** – Al rediseñar las moléculas en las interfaces, los científicos mejoraron mucho la potencia y durabilidad de las células solares de perovskita, acercándolas a un uso práctico.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of intricately interlocking geometric crystal structures symbolizing perovskite layers, with delicate, stylized molecular rings (pyridine and pyrimidine shapes) gracefully aligning flat atop the crystals, connected by glowing, subtle bonds representing dual Pb-N and Pb-I-π interactions, all set against a softly glowing backdrop that evokes stable, efficient energy flow without showing any human figures or text.

articles.title: Santa Marta May Be the Moment the World Started Walking Away From Fossil Fuels
articles.summary:
Fifty-seven countries representing roughly a third of the global economy walked into a coal port...
The post Santa Marta May Be the Moment the World Started Walking Away From Fossil Fuels appeared first on Earth911.
articles.content:
Fifty-seven countries representing roughly a third of the global economy walked into a coal port and agreed it was time to leave coal, oil, and gas behind. This is not the beginning of a joke. They did it without the United States, China, India, Russia, or Saudi Arabia in the room; and that was the point.

The First Conference on Transitioning Away from Fossil Fuels, co-hosted by Colombia and the Netherlands in Santa Marta, Colombia, from April 24 to 29, was conceived as an end-run petrostates that have stalled U.N. climate talks for three decades. It opened against the backdrop of the Iran war, the largest oil supply disruption in history, and a growing sense in capitals from Manila to Madrid that fossil fuel dependence is no longer just a climate problem, it is a national security concern.

Whether Santa Marta marks a genuine inflection point or another diplomatic detour will depend on what the participating governments do in the next 18 months. But the debate has shifted, and that matters to the climate and U.S. energy policy.

A Coalition of the Willing, Sitting in a Circle

The Santa Marta format was deliberately unlike a United Nations Conference of Parties, or COP. Instead of plenary speeches and bracketed text, ministers and envoys sat in small circles, discussing issues with civil society and Indigenous representatives in the room. Officials, according to Carbon Brief’s on-site reporting, described the conversations as “refreshing,” “highly successful,” and “groundbreaking.”

The guest list was as much a statement as the agenda. Colombia and the Netherlands invited countries that had backed a roadmap for a fossil fuel phase-out at COP30 in Belém last year. China, India, Russia, the United States, and the Gulf states were not on the list. Co-host Irene Vélez Torres, Colombia’s environment minister, told reporters the goal was to avoid “a rehashing” of Belém and to gather a “coalition of the willing.” Among those willing were several major fossil fuel producers, including Australia, Norway, Canada, Colombia itself, and Nigeria, which acknowledged the contradictions in their own economies but committed to the conversation.

Panama’s special climate representative, Juan Carlos Monterrey Gómez, speaking at the opening plenary, captured the mood: “For 34 years, we have negotiated the symptoms of the climate crisis and bulletproofed its cause. Thirty-four years of pledges. And where are we now? Economies built on fossil fuels are unraveling in real time. Fossil fuels are not just dirty. They are unreliable, they are dangerous, and they must end.”

The Iran War Changed the Game

The conference happened in the long shadow of the Iran war. The closure of the Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly 20 percent of global oil and significant LNG volumes pass, triggered what the International Energy Agency (IEA) has called the largest supply disruption in the history of the global oil market. Brent crude hit $144 per barrel earlier this spring. U.S. gasoline averaged $4.10 a gallon. The Philippines declared an energy emergency. Pakistan moved to a four-day public sector workweek to conserve fuel.

Those disruptions reframed the energy transition argument. UK climate envoy Rachel Kyte told Santa Marta delegates it “would be irresponsible to ignore the second fossil-fuel crisis in five years,” referring to the war in Ukraine and now Iran.

“Price volatility and dependence on imports are structurally and unacceptably impacting our economies,” Dutch climate minister Stientje van Veldhoven told the attendees. “We need to move away from fossil fuels not only because it is good for the climate, but because it strengthens our energy security.”

U.N. climate chief Simon Stiell made the same point earlier this spring at a meeting with the IEA in Paris, telling reporters that the war is “supercharging” the energy transition. The IEA reports that the Iran war has “thoroughly upended” the global outlook for oil consumption, with global demand now projected to contract by 80,000 barrels per day in 2026, the first annual decline since the 2020 pandemic. The IEA had projected growth of 730,000 barrels per day before the war began.

The shift is showing up in trade flows. Chinese exports of solar panels, batteries, and electric vehicles rose 70 percent year over year in March, according to energy think tank Ember, with EV exports up 140 percent.

“The era of fossil fuel security is over,” U.K. Energy Secretary Ed Miliband said in a statement that week. “the era of clean energy security must come of age.”

What Santa Marta Produced

Santa Marta wasn’t a treaty negotiation, and the co-hosts were clear that it would not produce binding commitments. What it did produce was a structure for making progress. The closing plenary on April 29 announced four concrete deliverables:

A second conference in 2027, co-hosted by Tuvalu and Ireland—an explicit pairing of a small island state and a high-income country to signal the coalition’s membership.

A workstream to develop national fossil fuel transition roadmaps, supported by a new global science panel. France and Colombia each released their own roadmaps during the conference.

A financial reform project focused on identifying fossil fuel subsidies and addressing the debt traps that constrain developing countries. Supported by the International Institute for Sustainable Development.

An effort to decarbonize trade, supported by the OECD, with the goal of building toward a “fossil fuel–free trade system.”

The new Science Panel for Global Energy Transition was launched at the academic pre-conference. It will be based at the University of São Paulo and will involve 50 to 100 scientists. Unlike the U.N.’s seven-year assessment cycle, the panel intends to produce annual updates and country-specific analysis on request. Johan Rockström of the Potsdam Institute and Carlos Nobre of the University of São Paulo, who launched it, framed the panel as deliberately independent of government line-by-line approval, which is a major change from the U.N. model.

A science pre-conference also produced a synthesis report from roughly 400 scientists with 12 “action insights,” including explicit recommendations to halt all new fossil fuel expansion and to prohibit fossil fuel advertising on the grounds that fossil fuels are health-harming products. A separate roadmap, led by Professor Piers Forster of the University of Leeds, outlined how Colombia could cut energy emissions to 90 percent below 2015 levels by 2050, with net economy-wide savings of about $23 billion annually by mid-century.

The Brazilian COP30 presidency has committed to building these inputs into an “informal” fossil fuel roadmap to be presented at COP31 in Turkey this November. That handoff is the test. Santa Marta produced a process; COP31 will reveal whether the process has political weight.

The Limits Of Cooperation

It would be easy to oversell this. Santa Marta gathered representatives of roughly a third of the global economy. The other two-thirds, including the world’s top two emitters and its largest oil producer, the United States, were absent. Tuvalu’s climate minister Maina Talia, who will co-host the 2027 conference, told Climate Home News that the criteria for invitations would have to change “If we are missing out the main players in the discussion, then we are moving in a loop, he said. “We need to find somehow how we can engage with [them], because there is no point in talking to ourselves.”

The Fossil Fuel Treaty initiative, a binding legal instrument that 18 nations have backed, did not appear in the final report. None of the workstreams has enforcement mechanisms. And the same Iran war that is accelerating renewable adoption is also being used by some governments, including the Trump administration, as justification to roll back climate policy and expand domestic fossil fuel production. Energy security can be argued in either direction. Which argument wins is a political fight, not a technical one.

Canada’s opening statement at the conference was widely noted for managing to avoid the words “fossil fuels” entirely—a reminder that even among the willing, willingness varies.

And outside the venue, Colombian mining unions protested the conference, holding signs that read “More oil, less Petro.” Colombia heads into a presidential election in late May, and President Gustavo Petro’s successor is not guaranteed. The durability of the Santa Marta process depends on a level of continuity that no single host country can guarantee.

Why It Still Matters

Santa Marta is not the moment fossil fuels ended. It is the moment a critical mass of governments stopped pretending the COP process alone could end them. That is a meaningful diplomatic shift. For three decades, the industry’s biggest structural advantage at U.N. talks has been the consensus rule: any single petrostate could block any binding language on production. Santa Marta is the first serious attempt to route around that veto.

The Strait of Hormuz crisis made it impossible for finance ministers, defense ministers, and central bankers to keep treating fossil fuel dependence as a separate file from national security. The IEA’s Fatih Birol called the situation the “greatest global energy security challenge in history.” Solar and battery costs that have fallen 80 percent and 90 percent, respectively, over the last decade made the alternative real. Santa Marta gave that combination a forum.

Whether the world is actually pivoting away from fossil fuels faster is something we will measure in pipeline cancellations, capital flows, and emissions curves over the next several years—not in conference communiqués. But the rhetorical floor moved in Santa Marta. “Fossil fuel” went from a phrase carefully edited out of negotiated text to the title of a conference that 57 governments showed up to. Coalitions of the doers tend to start small and either grow or fade. This one is worth watching.

What You Can Do

Individual action alone will not phase out fossil fuels. But the policy decisions that will, especially over the next 18 months heading into COP31, are shaped by sustained public pressure and personal choices that signal demand:

Track the workstreams. Santa Marta’s three workstreams (national roadmaps, finance, trade) and the Brazilian COP30 presidency’s informal fossil fuel roadmap will be the substantive deliverables to watch ahead of COP31. Climate Home News, Carbon Brief, and the Fossil Fuel Treaty initiative all publish detailed updates.

Santa Marta’s three workstreams (national roadmaps, finance, trade) and the Brazilian COP30 presidency’s informal fossil fuel roadmap will be the substantive deliverables to watch ahead of COP31. Climate Home News, Carbon Brief, and the Fossil Fuel Treaty initiative all publish detailed updates. Ask your representatives where they stand. In the U.S., neither party invited Santa Marta participants. State and city governments, however, can join subnational coalitions like the Beyond Oil and Gas Alliance. Local action remains the most practical lever.

In the U.S., neither party invited Santa Marta participants. State and city governments, however, can join subnational coalitions like the Beyond Oil and Gas Alliance. Local action remains the most practical lever. Reduce your own exposure to oil price volatility. Heat pumps, EVs, and rooftop or community solar are the household-scale equivalent of energy security policy. Federal tax credits remain available for many of these in 2026, though the IRA framework is under active threat—worth acting before that changes.

Heat pumps, EVs, and rooftop or community solar are the household-scale equivalent of energy security policy. Federal tax credits remain available for many of these in 2026, though the IRA framework is under active threat—worth acting before that changes. Support utilities and pension funds that are divesting from fossil fuels. Where you have a vote, whether as a customer, a shareholder, or a pension participant, ask whether the organization is screening for fossil fuel transition risk.

Where you have a vote, whether as a customer, a shareholder, or a pension participant, ask whether the organization is screening for fossil fuel transition risk. Donate or volunteer with groups doing transition work. The Fossil Fuel Non-Proliferation Treaty Initiative, Climate Action Network, and Indigenous-led organizations like the Organisation of Indigenous Peoples of the Colombian Amazon were central to making Santa Marta happen.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant diplomatic event where 57 countries, representing a substantial portion of the global economy, convened to commit to transitioning away from fossil fuels. This initiative marks a meaningful shift in global climate action and energy security policy, with concrete deliverables such as national roadmaps, financial reforms, and trade decarbonization efforts. The event's impact is broad, involving multiple governments and scientific communities, and it addresses a critical global challenge with tangible plans and ongoing processes.
articles.personality_title: Fifty-seven countries unite to plan a future without fossil fuels
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For decades, global talks on climate change have struggled to agree on how to reduce the use of fossil fuels like coal, oil, and gas. Many powerful countries have blocked progress, making it hard to find solutions that everyone supports.

**What happened** – In April 2024, 57 countries representing about one-third of the world's economy met in Santa Marta, Colombia, to discuss moving away from fossil fuels. This meeting was different because it was smaller, focused, and did not include some of the biggest fossil fuel users like the United States, China, India, and Russia. The conference encouraged open talks between governments, scientists, and Indigenous groups, leading to new ideas and plans.

**Impact** – This meeting is important because it shows a new way to make progress on climate change without waiting for all countries to agree. The group created clear steps for the future: a follow-up conference in 2027, plans to help countries create their own fossil fuel phase-out roadmaps, efforts to change financial rules that support fossil fuels, and ways to make global trade less dependent on oil and gas. The event also launched a new science panel to provide yearly updates on energy transition progress.

**What's next step** – The success of this approach depends on what happens in the next 18 months leading to the United Nations climate conference (COP31) in Turkey in November 2024. The world will watch if these plans influence global decisions and if more countries join the effort. Meanwhile, individuals and local governments can support the transition by choosing cleaner energy options and encouraging their leaders to take part.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A group of 57 countries has started a new, focused effort to move away from fossil fuels with clear plans and cooperation, signaling a shift in how the world tackles climate change and energy security.
articles.personality_title_fr: 57 pays s’unissent pour planifier un avenir sans combustibles fossiles
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis des décennies, les discussions mondiales sur le changement climatique peinent à trouver un accord pour réduire l’utilisation des combustibles fossiles comme le charbon, le pétrole et le gaz. De nombreux pays puissants bloquent les progrès, rendant les solutions difficiles à mettre en place.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En avril 2024, 57 pays représentant environ un tiers de l’économie mondiale se sont réunis à Santa Marta, en Colombie, pour discuter de la sortie des combustibles fossiles. Cette réunion était différente car elle était plus petite, ciblée, et n’incluait pas certains des plus grands utilisateurs comme les États-Unis, la Chine, l’Inde et la Russie. La conférence a favorisé des échanges ouverts entre gouvernements, scientifiques et groupes autochtones, aboutissant à de nouvelles idées et plans.

**Impact** – Cette rencontre est importante car elle montre une nouvelle façon de progresser sur le climat sans attendre l’accord de tous les pays. Le groupe a créé des étapes claires pour l’avenir : une nouvelle conférence en 2027, des plans pour aider chaque pays à élaborer sa propre feuille de route pour sortir des combustibles fossiles, des efforts pour réformer les règles financières qui soutiennent ces énergies, et des moyens pour rendre le commerce mondial moins dépendant du pétrole et du gaz. Un nouveau panel scientifique a aussi été lancé pour fournir des mises à jour annuelles sur la transition énergétique.

**Prochaine étape** – Le succès de cette approche dépendra des actions dans les 18 prochains mois avant la conférence climatique des Nations Unies (COP31) en Turquie en novembre 2024. Le monde observera si ces plans influencent les décisions mondiales et si davantage de pays rejoignent l’effort. En attendant, les citoyens et gouvernements locaux peuvent soutenir la transition en choisissant des énergies plus propres et en encourageant leurs dirigeants à participer.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un groupe de 57 pays a lancé un nouvel effort ciblé pour sortir des combustibles fossiles avec des plans clairs et une coopération, marquant un changement dans la lutte mondiale contre le changement climatique et pour la sécurité énergétique.
articles.personality_title_es: 57 países se unen para planear un futuro sin combustibles fósiles
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante décadas, las negociaciones globales sobre el cambio climático han tenido dificultades para ponerse de acuerdo en cómo reducir el uso de combustibles fósiles como el carbón, el petróleo y el gas. Muchos países poderosos han bloqueado el progreso, dificultando encontrar soluciones aceptadas por todos.

**Qué pasó** – En abril de 2024, 57 países que representan alrededor de un tercio de la economía mundial se reunieron en Santa Marta, Colombia, para discutir cómo dejar atrás los combustibles fósiles. Esta reunión fue diferente porque fue más pequeña, enfocada y no incluyó a algunos de los mayores consumidores como Estados Unidos, China, India y Rusia. La conferencia promovió conversaciones abiertas entre gobiernos, científicos y grupos indígenas, generando nuevas ideas y planes.

**Impacto** – Esta reunión es importante porque muestra una nueva forma de avanzar en el cambio climático sin esperar el acuerdo de todos los países. El grupo creó pasos claros para el futuro: una segunda conferencia en 2027, planes para ayudar a los países a crear sus propias hojas de ruta para dejar los combustibles fósiles, esfuerzos para cambiar las reglas financieras que apoyan estos combustibles y maneras de hacer que el comercio mundial dependa menos del petróleo y el gas. También se lanzó un nuevo panel científico para entregar actualizaciones anuales sobre el progreso en la transición energética.

**Próximo paso** – El éxito de este enfoque dependerá de lo que ocurra en los próximos 18 meses antes de la conferencia climática de la ONU (COP31) en Turquía en noviembre de 2024. El mundo observará si estos planes influyen en las decisiones globales y si más países se unen al esfuerzo. Mientras tanto, las personas y gobiernos locales pueden apoyar la transición eligiendo opciones de energía más limpia y animando a sus líderes a participar.

**Resumen en una frase** – Un grupo de 57 países ha iniciado un nuevo esfuerzo enfocado para dejar atrás los combustibles fósiles con planes claros y cooperación, señalando un cambio en cómo el mundo enfrenta el cambio climático y la seguridad energética.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a diverse circle of glowing, interconnected globes representing 57 countries, set on a peaceful coastal landscape symbolizing Santa Marta, Colombia, with fading dark smoke clouds on one side and rising bright solar panels and wind turbines on the other, all rendered in soft natural earth tones and gentle blues.

articles.title: Best of Sustainability In Your Ear: Algenesis & Blueview Launch the Algae-Based Polyurethane Industry
articles.summary:
Travel back in time to hear the origin story of Algenesis, which started as two...
The post Best of Sustainability In Your Ear: Algenesis & Blueview Launch the Algae-Based Polyurethane Industry appeared first on Earth911.
articles.content:
Travel back in time to hear the origin story of Algenesis, which started as two companies in one, a biotechnology innovator and footwear maker. Today, the company is a leading maker of bio-based plastics. In 2023, Algenesis had just begun making a new, sustainable material and found a clever way to prove its utility to get big companies to embrace it. Join the conversation hear why a shoe company was the best a practical application to prove the value of a plant-based, compostable bioplastic foam. Stephen Mayfield, a professor of Biology at UC San Diego and director of the California Center for Algae Biotechnology, invented Soleic, an algae-based rubbery foam material that can be used in footwear, surfboards, and other products in the place of petroleum-based polyurethane foam. He launched Algenesis, a biotechnology-based materials science company to commercialize Soleic.

Note: This article contains affiliate links that help fund our Recycling Directory, the most comprehensive in North America.

But shoe companies did not come running to use Soleic, which biodegrades completely in sea water and compost piles. Along with Algenisis president Tom Cooke, a footwear and apparel industry veteran who had worked for Reef and Vans, Steve launched Blueview Footwear, maker of the world’s first compostable shoe. Steve and Tom join me today to talk about the evolution of Algenesis and Blueview, as well as the many materials Soleic could replace across a variety of product categories. The companies have also developed compostable, plant-based fabrics and a bioplastic waterproofing technology that biodegrades into organic material in a home compost pile. You can learn more about Blueview Footwear at blueviewfootwear.com and its parent company Algenesis Materials at algenesismaterials.com.

Subscribe to Sustainability in Your Ear on iTunes and Apple Podcasts.

Follow Sustainability in Your Ear on Spreaker, iHeartRadio, or YouTube.

Editor’s Note: This podcast originally aired on February 20, 2023.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant sustainable innovation: the development and commercialization of Soleic, an algae-based, compostable bioplastic foam that can replace petroleum-based polyurethane foam. This innovation has broad environmental benefits by reducing plastic pollution and reliance on fossil fuels. The story includes concrete details about the technology, its applications, and the companies involved, demonstrating a meaningful positive impact on sustainability and materials science.
articles.personality_title: Algenesis and Blueview launch algae-based foam to replace plastic in shoes
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Algenesis started as a combination of a biotech company and a shoe maker, aiming to create new, eco-friendly materials. Many plastics today come from fossil fuels and don’t break down easily, causing pollution.

**What happened** – In 2023, Algenesis introduced Soleic, a foam made from algae that can replace traditional plastic foam used in shoes and other products. This foam is special because it completely breaks down in seawater and compost. To show how useful Soleic is, Algenesis and Blueview Footwear created the world’s first compostable shoe using this algae-based foam.

**Impact** – This innovation could reduce plastic waste and pollution by offering a natural, plant-based alternative to common plastics in shoes and other goods. Because Soleic breaks down safely in the environment, it helps protect oceans and land from leftover plastic.

**What's next step** – Algenesis and Blueview plan to expand the use of Soleic into more products beyond shoes, including fabrics and waterproof materials that also compost naturally. They hope more companies will adopt these materials to reduce plastic pollution.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Algenesis and Blueview have created a compostable shoe foam from algae that offers a sustainable alternative to plastic, helping reduce pollution.
articles.personality_title_fr: Algenesis et Blueview lancent une mousse à base d’algues pour remplacer le plastique dans les chaussures
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Algenesis a commencé comme une combinaison d’une entreprise de biotechnologie et d’un fabricant de chaussures, visant à créer de nouveaux matériaux écologiques. Beaucoup de plastiques actuels viennent des combustibles fossiles et ne se décomposent pas facilement, ce qui cause de la pollution.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2023, Algenesis a présenté Soleic, une mousse faite d’algues qui peut remplacer la mousse plastique traditionnelle utilisée dans les chaussures et d’autres produits. Cette mousse est spéciale car elle se décompose complètement dans l’eau de mer et le compost. Pour montrer l’utilité de Soleic, Algenesis et Blueview Footwear ont créé la première chaussure compostable au monde utilisant cette mousse à base d’algues.

**Impact** – Cette innovation pourrait réduire les déchets plastiques et la pollution en offrant une alternative naturelle et végétale aux plastiques courants dans les chaussures et autres produits. Comme Soleic se décompose naturellement, il aide à protéger les océans et la terre des déchets plastiques.

**Prochaines étapes** – Algenesis et Blueview prévoient d’étendre l’utilisation de Soleic à plus de produits au-delà des chaussures, y compris des tissus et des matériaux imperméables qui se compostent aussi naturellement. Ils espèrent que plus d’entreprises adopteront ces matériaux pour réduire la pollution plastique.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Algenesis et Blueview ont créé une mousse compostable à base d’algues qui offre une alternative durable au plastique, aidant à réduire la pollution.
articles.personality_title_es: Algenesis y Blueview lanzan espuma de algas para reemplazar el plástico en zapatos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Algenesis comenzó como una combinación de una empresa biotecnológica y un fabricante de calzado, con el objetivo de crear materiales nuevos y ecológicos. Muchos plásticos hoy provienen de combustibles fósiles y no se descomponen fácilmente, causando contaminación.

**Qué pasó** – En 2023, Algenesis presentó Soleic, una espuma hecha de algas que puede reemplazar la espuma plástica tradicional usada en zapatos y otros productos. Esta espuma es especial porque se descompone completamente en agua de mar y compost. Para demostrar la utilidad de Soleic, Algenesis y Blueview Footwear crearon el primer zapato compostable del mundo usando esta espuma a base de algas.

**Impacto** – Esta innovación podría reducir los desechos plásticos y la contaminación al ofrecer una alternativa natural y vegetal a los plásticos comunes en zapatos y otros productos. Como Soleic se descompone de forma segura, ayuda a proteger los océanos y la tierra de residuos plásticos.

**Próximo paso** – Algenesis y Blueview planean expandir el uso de Soleic a más productos, incluyendo telas y materiales impermeables que también se compostan naturalmente. Esperan que más empresas adopten estos materiales para reducir la contaminación plástica.

**Frase clave** – Algenesis y Blueview han creado una espuma compostable de algas que ofrece una alternativa sostenible al plástico, ayudando a reducir la contaminación.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a pair of eco-friendly shoes crafted from soft, textured algae-based foam resting gently on a shoreline where clear seawater meets composting earth, with subtle silhouettes of surfboards and fabric rolls woven from plant-based materials blending into the natural background, all rendered in gentle, natural tones of green, beige, and soft blue.
